per_example.claim: The case that The Freedom of movement under United States law held consistently in 1873 and United States v. Darby Lumber Co. did not both involve the same law.
supporting_facts: United States v. Darby Lumber Co.
Freedom of movement under United States law
per_example.summary_1: Freedom of movement under United States law is governed primarily by the Privileges and Immunities Clause of the U.S. Constitution, with judicial recognition dating back to Corfield v. Coryell (1823). The Supreme Court held consistently through cases such as Ward v. Maryland (1871), the Slaughter-House Cases (1873), and United States v. Harris (1883) that freedom of movement is a fundamental constitutional right but that the federal government does not have authority to protect it, leaving that to the states. United States v. Darby Lumber Co. (1941) upheld the Fair Labor Standards Act under the Commerce Clause, regulating employment conditions, and is unrelated to freedom of movement law.
per_example.summary_2: The new passages provide detailed information about the Slaughter-House Cases (1873), highlighting that it was the first Supreme Court interpretation of the Fourteenth Amendment's Privileges or Immunities Clause, which limited federal protection of rights to a narrow set including the right to travel between states. This reinforces that the 1873 case involved constitutional law concerning freedom of movement under the Fourteenth Amendment. The passages do not discuss United States v. Darby Lumber Co., which the context states involved the Fair Labor Standards Act under the Commerce Clause and is unrelated to freedom of movement law. Thus, the passages support the claim that the Slaughter-House Cases and United States v. Darby Lumber Co. did not involve the same law.
per_example.hop2_query: Slaughter-House Cases law involved
per_example.hop3_query: United States v. Darby Lumber Co. legal basis
